title: Describing Data
blurb: Center and spread done right: mean, median, IQR, standard deviation, boxplots, and the 1.5×IQR outlier fence.
buildsOn: Expected value (Unit 7) — the mean of data is expectation seen from below.

lessons.title: Two Centers: Mean & Median
lessons.concreteComparison: Nine villagers earn about $1$ million ₮ a month; then a mining executive moves in at $91$ million. The village's MEAN income rockets to $10$ million — a number describing nobody. The MEDIAN stays at $1$ million — describing almost everybody. Two honest centers, two different questions: where does the total balance, and where does the typical person stand?
lessons.objective: Compute mean and median, understand each one's relationship to the data, and predict how outliers move them.
concept: The **mean** $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{n}$ is the balance point of the data — Unit 7's expected value computed FROM observations instead of probabilities. Every value pulls on it, proportionally to its distance.
The **median** is the middle of the SORTED list ($n$ odd) or the average of the two middles ($n$ even) — a positional center: half the data on each side.

**Outlier response** separates them: the executive drags the mean by millions but leaves the median untouched (he's just one value on the right, wherever he sits). The mean is SENSITIVE; the median is RESISTANT.

Both are honest; they answer different questions. Totals and fair-shares → mean (a village fundraiser cares about the total). Typical member → median (a family choosing a neighborhood cares where the middle lives). Mismatched center-to-question is how statistics mislead without lying.
lessons.keyIdea: Mean = balance point (every value pulls); median = positional middle (resistant). Pick the center that answers the question asked.

facts.title: The two centers
facts.explanation: Balance point versus positional middle — sensitivity versus resistance.

facts.title: Outlier response
facts.explanation: One extreme value shifts the mean by (its excess)/n and the median by at most one position.

workedExamples.statement: Village incomes (millions ₮): nine values of $1$ and one of $91$. Compute both centers.
workedExamples.solution: Mean: $\frac{9 \times 1 + 91}{10} = \frac{100}{10} = 10$ million. Median: sorted, the $5$th and $6$th values are both $1$ → median $= 1$ million. The mean answers "total ÷ heads"; the median answers "the typical villager".

workedExamples.statement: Quiz scores: $6, 9, 4, 7, 9$. Find the mean and the median.
workedExamples.solution: Mean: $\frac{6+9+4+7+9}{5} = \frac{35}{5} = 7$. Sorted: $4, 6, 7, 9, 9$ — median $= 7$ (the third value). Centers agree here: no extreme values, roughly symmetric data.

workedExamples.statement: Delivery times (min): $22, 25, 27, 30$. Find the median, then recompute both centers after one disaster delivery of $110$ minutes joins the data.
workedExamples.solution: Median of four: $\frac{25 + 27}{2} = 26$. With $110$: mean jumps from $26$ to $\frac{104 + 110}{5} = 42.8$; sorted $22, 25, 27, 30, 110$ → median moves only to $27$. One bad day rewrote the mean and barely nudged the median.

commonMistakes.correction: The median of 6, 9, 4, 7, 9 is NOT 4 (the middle of the unsorted list). Sort first: 4 6 7 9 9 → 7. Every time.

commonMistakes.text: Reporting the mean for skewed data without comment.
commonMistakes.correction: "Average income: 10 million" describes a village where nobody earns that. With outliers or skew, report the median — or both, labeled.

tryIt.statement: Data: $10, 12, 11, 95$. Which center better describes a typical value, and what are both?
tryIt.solution: Mean $= \frac{128}{4} = 32$ (dragged by $95$); median $= \frac{11 + 12}{2} = 11.5$. The median — three of four values sit within $1$ of it.

steps.title: The executive moves in
beats: Nine incomes of $1$M₮... then one of $91$M₮ arrives.
Mean: $10$M₮ — describes NOBODY.
Median: $1$M₮ — describes almost everybody.
Two centers, two questions. Choosing wrong is how numbers lie.

steps.title: Balance vs position
beats: Mean: sum over count — the BALANCE POINT. Unit 7's expectation, from data.
Median: SORT, take the middle (average two middles if $n$ even).
Every value pulls the mean; only ORDER matters to the median.
Sensitive vs resistant — remember which is which by the executive.

steps.title: Pull one value, watch who follows
steps.teach: Six quiz scores. Push the movable dot to the right with the stepper — the **mean** chases it, because every point pulls on the balance triangle. The **median** barely moves: it only cares about the middle of the ordered list. That's robustness, and it's why one weird value can wreck an average.

steps.title: Median drill
steps.explanation: Sorted: $2, 4, 6, 7, 9$ — middle is $6$. The $7$ reads the unsorted middle; $5.6$ is the MEAN sneaking into a median question.

steps.title: Which center answers it?
steps.prompt: A charity wants to predict TOTAL donations from $500$ donors like last year's. Which center of last year's donations should it use?
options: The mean — totals come from means: total = mean × n
steps.explanation: Total = mean × count, by definition — the mean's sensitivity to big donors is exactly what a TOTAL needs to include. The median would ignore the whales who fund half the budget. Resistant isn't always right; match the center to the question.

steps.title: Ask which 'average' they used
steps.body: "Average" in headlines is usually the mean — and in incomes, house prices, and net worth (right-skewed everything), the mean runs well above the typical person. When a statistic surprises you, your first question is now professional: mean or median, and who benefits from the choice?

steps.title: The billionaire on the bus
steps.body: Statisticians joke: when a billionaire boards a city bus, the passengers' MEAN wealth exceeds a hundred million — and every single rider is still worrying about rent. The median doesn't get the joke, which is precisely its virtue.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Mean = balance point (Σx/n); median = sorted middle.
Outliers drag the mean, barely touch the median.
Match the center to the question: totals → mean, typical → median.

lessons.title: Quartiles & the Five-Number Summary
lessons.concreteComparison: Two classes both have median test score $70$. Class A's scores huddle between $65$ and $75$; Class B's sprawl from $30$ to $100$. The median can't tell them apart — you need to know where the MIDDLE HALF of each class lives. Quartiles cut the sorted data into four equal chunks, and the distance between the cuts is the spread that outliers can't fake.
lessons.objective: Find quartiles and the five-number summary, and compute the IQR as a resistant measure of spread.
concept: **Quartiles** cut sorted data into quarters: $Q_1$ (median of the lower half), $Q_2$ (the median itself), $Q_3$ (median of the upper half). Convention: when $n$ is odd, the overall median is left OUT of both halves.
The **five-number summary** — min, $Q_1$, median, $Q_3$, max — sketches the whole distribution in five values: where it starts, where each quarter lives, where it ends.
The **interquartile range** $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ spans the middle $50\%$ of the data. Like the median, it's RESISTANT: the executive's $91$ million changes the max, not the quartiles.

Range ($\max - \min$) is the quick-and-dirty spread — one subtraction, but the two most extreme (least trustworthy) values are its only inputs. IQR trades a little more work for immunity to exactly those values.
lessons.keyIdea: Q1, median, Q3 cut sorted data into quarters; IQR = Q3 − Q1 measures the middle half's span — spread that outliers can't distort.

facts.title: Five-number summary
facts.explanation: The distribution's skeleton in five sorted landmarks.

facts.title: IQR
facts.explanation: The span of the middle 50% — resistant, unlike the range.

workedExamples.statement: Scores: $4, 7, 8, 10, 12, 15, 21$ (already sorted, $n = 7$). Find the five-number summary and IQR.
Class A: $65, 67, 69, 71, 73, 75$. Class B: $30, 50, 65, 75, 90, 100$. Both medians are $70$. Compare IQRs.
workedExamples.solution: A: $Q_1 = 67, Q_3 = 73$ (medians of $\{65,67,69\}$ and $\{71,73,75\}$), IQR $= 6$. B: $Q_1 = 50, Q_3 = 90$, IQR $= 40$. Same center, spreads differing by a factor of nearly $7$ — the middle half tells the story the median hid.

workedExamples.statement: Delivery times: $22, 25, 27, 30, 110$. Compare the range and the IQR as spread reports.
workedExamples.solution: Range: $110 - 22 = 88$ — dominated by one disaster. Quartiles ($n = 5$, median $27$ excluded): lower $\{22, 25\} \to Q_1 = 23.5$; upper $\{30, 110\} \to Q_3 = 70$... note even $Q_3$ feels the outlier with tiny data. With realistic $n$, the IQR ignores the disaster entirely; the range NEVER does — it's built from the two worst-behaved values.

commonMistakes.correction: Quartiles are POSITIONS in the sorted list. Sort first — same discipline as the median, doubly important now.
IQR is ONE number — the width Q3 − Q1 — not the pair (Q1, Q3). "IQR = 8" is correct; "IQR = 7 to 15" confuses the span with its endpoints.

tryIt.statement: Sorted data: $2, 5, 6, 8, 9, 12, 14, 18$ ($n = 8$). Find $Q_1$, the median, $Q_3$, and IQR.
In dd-l2-t1's data, the $18$ becomes $180$. Which of range and IQR change?
tryIt.solution: Range: $18 - 2 = 16$ becomes $178$ — exploded. IQR: quartiles unmoved (the $180$ is still just 'the largest value') — IQR stays $7.5$. Resistance, demonstrated.

steps.title: Same median, different classes
beats: Class A: scores huddle $65$–$75$. Class B: sprawl $30$–$100$.
Both medians: $70$. The center is blind to the difference.
The question that separates them: where does the MIDDLE HALF live?
Quartiles answer it.

steps.title: Quarters of the sorted list
beats: $Q_2$ = the median. $Q_1$ = median of the lower half. $Q_3$ = of the upper.
Odd $n$: the overall median joins NEITHER half.
Five-number summary: min, $Q_1$, med, $Q_3$, max.
IQR $= Q_3 - Q_1$: the middle 50%'s span. One number.

steps.title: Five numbers, one picture
steps.teach: Eight values, summarized by five numbers. Tap each chip: min and max are the whisker tips, the box runs from $Q_1$ to $Q_3$, and the heavy line inside is the median. The box's width is the IQR — the span of the middle half of the data, immune to whatever the extremes are doing.

steps.title: Find Q1
steps.explanation: Median is $9$ (4th); lower half is $\{3, 6, 7\}$ (median excluded) — its middle is $6$. Including the median in the half (getting $\{3,6,7,9\} \to 6.5$) is the classic convention slip.

steps.title: What moves?
steps.prompt: In a dataset of $40$ house prices, the top price doubles. Which statistics change?
options: Mean, range, max — but not median, Q1, Q3, IQR
steps.explanation: The doubled top is still just 'the largest value': every positional statistic (median, quartiles, IQR) is untouched. The mean absorbs the increase ÷ 40, and the range/max take it in full.

steps.title: Software may disagree slightly
steps.body: There are several quartile conventions (exclude-the-median, include-it, interpolation…) and spreadsheets pick their own — small datasets can give slightly different Q1/Q3 across tools. The concept (middle of each half) is universal; on an exam, use the course convention: odd n excludes the median from both halves.

steps.title: Quartiles run your paycheck
steps.body: Salary negotiations quote 'the 25th–75th percentile band for this role' — literally Q1 to Q3 of market data. HR departments prefer quartiles over means for exactly this unit's reason: one celebrity hire would otherwise drag every 'average salary' benchmark in the company.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Quartiles: medians of the halves (odd n: overall median excluded).
Five-number summary sketches the whole distribution.
IQR = Q3 − Q1: one resistant number; range is fast but fragile.

lessons.title: Boxplots & the Outlier Fence
lessons.concreteComparison: When is a slow delivery just slow, and when is it an OUTLIER? Eyeballing invites arguments. The standard rule draws a fence: anything beyond $1.5 \times \text{IQR}$ outside the quartiles is flagged. The boxplot draws the whole verdict — box for the middle half, whiskers to the last inliers, dots for the flagged — one picture, five numbers, and a fence.
lessons.objective: Draw and read boxplots, apply the 1.5×IQR fence to flag outliers, and compare groups side by side.
concept: **The fence rule:** compute $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$; the fences sit at $Q_1 - 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5 \times \text{IQR}$. Values beyond either fence are flagged as **outliers** — a standard, arguable-but-agreed convention (not a law of nature).

A **boxplot** draws the five-number summary: a box from $Q_1$ to $Q_3$ with a line at the median, whiskers reaching to the most extreme values still INSIDE the fences, and individual dots for outliers beyond.
Reading one: the box is the middle half; a median line off-center in its box, or one whisker much longer, signals SKEW toward the long side.
Side-by-side boxplots are the comparison tool: two groups' centers, spreads, skews, and outliers in one glance — the picture Unit 12 will lean on to judge whether groups genuinely differ.
lessons.keyIdea: Fences at Q1 − 1.5·IQR and Q3 + 1.5·IQR flag outliers; the boxplot draws box, median, whiskers-to-last-inlier, and outlier dots.

facts.title: The fence rule
facts.explanation: Beyond either fence = flagged outlier, by convention.

facts.title: Whisker rule
facts.explanation: Not to the fences themselves, and not to outliers — to the most extreme inliers.

workedExamples.statement: Deliveries: five-number summary $22, 24, 27, 32, 110$ (with $Q_1 = 24$, $Q_3 = 32$). Fence the outliers.
workedExamples.solution: IQR $= 8$; $1.5 \times 8 = 12$. Fences: $24 - 12 = 12$ and $32 + 12 = 44$. The $110$ lies far beyond $44$: flagged. Everything else is inside. The boxplot shows the box $24$–$32$, whisker down to $22$, whisker up to the last inlier ($\le 44$), and a lone dot at $110$.

workedExamples.statement: Test scores: $Q_1 = 60$, median $= 75$, $Q_3 = 82$. What does the median's position inside the box say about shape?
workedExamples.solution: The median sits $15$ above $Q_1$ but only $7$ below $Q_3$ — the LOWER half of the box is stretched: the data reach further down than up. Left (low-score) skew: a tail of struggling scores below a bunched top.

workedExamples.statement: Plants under two fertilizers, heights (cm) as five-number summaries — A: $12, 15, 18, 21, 24$; B: $10, 18, 22, 24, 26$. Compare the groups from the (mental) side-by-side boxplots.
workedExamples.solution: Centers: B's median $22 > $ A's $18$. Spreads: IQR$_A = 6$, IQR$_B = 6$ — equal middles. Shape: A symmetric (median centered); B left-skewed (median nearer $Q_3$; long lower whisker to $10$). Verdict: B grows taller on the whole, with a straggler tail A doesn't have.

commonMistakes.text: Drawing whiskers to the fences (or to the outliers).
commonMistakes.correction: Whiskers reach the most extreme DATA VALUES still inside the fences — actual observations, not the fence positions, and never past a flagged point.

The fence FLAGS, it doesn't delete. A flagged point may be a typo (fix it), a different population (investigate), or a real extreme (keep it and say so). Flag → investigate → decide, with a paper trail.

tryIt.statement: Data with $Q_1 = 50$, $Q_3 = 70$: compute the fences, and judge the values $15$ and $95$.
tryIt.solution: IQR $= 20$; fences at $50 - 30 = 20$ and $70 + 30 = 100$. The $15$ is below $20$: flagged. The $95$ is inside: extreme-ish but legal.

tryIt.statement: A boxplot's upper whisker is three times the lower one, median hugging $Q_1$. Describe the shape.
tryIt.solution: Right (high-side) skew: the data stretch far upward; the bottom half is compressed. Typical for incomes, waiting times, house prices.

steps.title: Ending the eyeball argument
beats: "That delivery's an outlier!" "No, just slow!" — arguments need a rule.
The rule: fences at $Q_1 - 1.5\,\text{IQR}$ and $Q_3 + 1.5\,\text{IQR}$.
Beyond a fence → flagged. Inside → ordinary, even if extreme-ish.
A convention, agreed for a century — not a law of nature.

steps.title: Anatomy of a boxplot
beats: Box: $Q_1$ to $Q_3$ — the middle half. Line inside: the median.
Whiskers: out to the most extreme values still INSIDE the fences.
Dots beyond: the flagged outliers, drawn individually.
Five numbers + fence verdicts, one glance.

steps.title: The fence catches the 30
steps.teach: This is the lesson's own dataset: $5, 6, 6, 7, 8, 9, 30$. Toggle the fences: $Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR = 1.5$ and $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR = 13.5$. Everything must live between them — the 30 doesn't, so it's flagged in red and the whisker retreats to the last value inside. The rule is mechanical on purpose: no arguing about what "looks" extreme.

steps.title: Flag or shrug?
options: Yes — the upper fence is $42 + 18 = 60$... just AT the fence: not beyond, so no flag
No — outliers must exceed twice the max
steps.explanation: IQR $= 12$, upper fence $= 42 + 1.5 \times 12 = 60$: the rule flags values BEYOND the fence, and $60$ sits exactly on it — inside, technically. (Being above $Q_3$ describes a quarter of all data — never a flag by itself.)

steps.title: The flagged point
steps.prompt: A fenced outlier turns out to be a data-entry typo ($1100$ for $110$). The right move is…
options: Fix it to the verified true value and document the change
Delete it silently — it's flagged anyway
Keep it as-is — data is data
steps.explanation: Flags start investigations, not deletions. Verified errors get corrected WITH a note; unverifiable extremes get reported both ways (with/without). Silent deletion is how fraudulent-looking research is made.

steps.title: Compare boxplots in a fixed order
steps.body: Side-by-side boxplots: read CENTERS (medians), then SPREADS (box widths), then SHAPES (median position, whisker balance), then OUTLIERS. Four steps, spoken in that order, and no feature gets missed. This becomes the group-comparison ritual of Unit 12.

steps.title: Tukey invented it on paper
steps.body: John Tukey introduced the boxplot in 1977 — designed to be drawn BY HAND with five numbers and a pencil, part of his campaign for 'exploratory data analysis': look at data before modeling it. The 1.5 multiplier? Tukey reportedly said 1 was too small and 2 too large. Sometimes conventions are exactly that human.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Fences: quartiles ± 1.5·IQR; beyond = flagged, at = inside.
Boxplot: box, median line, whiskers to last inliers, dots beyond.
Flag → investigate → decide (documented); compare groups in fixed order.

lessons.title: Standard Deviation of Data
lessons.concreteComparison: Two archers each average $8$ points a shot. Ana's arrows: $7, 8, 8, 9$ — a tight cluster. Bat's: $4, 6, 10, 12$ — all over the target. The number that captures 'typical distance from the mean' is the standard deviation — Unit 7's $\sigma$, computed from observations — and it's about $0.7$ for Ana versus $3.2$ for Bat. Same average, four times the scatter.
lessons.objective: Compute the standard deviation of a dataset, interpret it as typical distance from the mean, and know when it (vs IQR) is the right spread.
concept: The recipe mirrors Unit 7 exactly: deviations $x_i - \bar{x}$, squared, averaged, rooted: $$s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n - 1}}$$ The $n - 1$ (not $n$) is the standard SAMPLE convention — a small correction because the sample mean is measured from the same data. (With the whole population, divide by $n$.)
**Interpretation** is the same as ever: $s$ is the typical distance of an observation from the mean, in the data's own units. Ana: arrows typically land $\sim 0.8$ from her average; Bat: $\sim 3.7$.

$s$ pairs with the MEAN (both sensitive, both use every value); IQR pairs with the MEDIAN (both resistant). Report matched pairs: mean ± SD for symmetric data, median + IQR when skew or outliers rule.
Anchors: $s = 0$ means all values identical; adding a constant to every value leaves $s$ unchanged (spread doesn't shift); scaling every value by $k$ scales $s$ by $|k|$ — Unit 7's rules, inherited.
lessons.keyIdea: s = √[Σ(x−x̄)²/(n−1)]: typical distance from the mean. Pair mean↔SD and median↔IQR — sensitive with sensitive, resistant with resistant.

facts.title: Sample SD
facts.explanation: Deviations squared, averaged with the n−1 convention, rooted back to data units.

facts.title: Matched pairs
facts.explanation: Sensitive center with sensitive spread; resistant with resistant.

workedExamples.solution: $\bar{x} = 8$. Deviations: $-1, 0, 0, 1$; squares sum to $2$. $s = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \approx 0.82$. Her arrows typically land less than a point from her average.

workedExamples.statement: Bat's arrows: $4, 6, 10, 12$. Compute $\bar{x}$ and $s$, and compare with Ana.
workedExamples.solution: $\bar{x} = 8$ — same as Ana. Deviations: $-4, -2, 2, 4$; squares sum to $40$. $s = \sqrt{\frac{40}{3}} \approx 3.65$. Same mean, four-and-a-half times Ana's scatter — the SD is the entire difference between these archers.

workedExamples.statement: A dataset has $\bar{x} = 50$, $s = 4$. Every value gets $10$ added (a scoring correction), then everything is doubled. Track $\bar{x}$ and $s$.
workedExamples.solution: Add $10$: mean $60$, SD unchanged at $4$ (shifting moves everyone together). Double: mean $120$, SD $8$ (scaling stretches gaps). Final: $\bar{x} = 120$, $s = 8$ — Unit 7's transformation rules, acting on data.

commonMistakes.correction: Deviations from the mean sum to zero, always — that's the mean's definition at work. Square, average, root: the order is the formula.

commonMistakes.text: Pairing the median with the SD (or mean with IQR).
commonMistakes.correction: Mismatched pairs mix sensitivity: an outlier then distorts one half of your report and not the other. Mean ± SD, or median + IQR — matched sets.

tryIt.statement: Data: $2, 4, 6, 8$. Compute $\bar{x}$ and the squared-deviation sum, then $s$.
Which dataset has the larger SD (no computing): $A = 10, 10, 10, 10$ or $B = 9, 10, 10, 11$?
tryIt.solution: $A$ has $s = 0$ (identical values); $B$ has any spread at all — $B$ wins. Zero SD means literally no variation.

steps.title: Same average, different targets
beats: Ana's typical miss: under a point. Bat's: over three.
The number for 'typical distance from the mean': the SD.
Unit 7's σ, computed from arrows instead of probabilities.

steps.title: Deviate, square, average, root
beats: Deviations: $x_i - \bar{x}$ (they sum to zero — squaring is mandatory).
Square them. Average with $n - 1$ (the sample convention).
Root back to data units: $s$.
Why $n-1$? The sample mean was fit FROM this data — one degree of honesty spent.

steps.title: Deviations you can point at
steps.teach: Every stick runs from the mean to a data point — green above, red below. The standard deviation is the **typical stick length**: square them, average, take the root. Tap any dot to read its deviation. For this set the answer is exactly $\sigma = 2$.

steps.title: The squared sum
steps.prompt: Data $1, 3, 5$ ($\bar{x} = 3$): the sum of squared deviations is…
steps.explanation: Deviations $-2, 0, 2$: squares $4, 0, 4$ sum to $8$. The $0$ is what UNsquared deviations give (always); $4$ takes only one arm.

steps.title: Report card
steps.prompt: House prices in a district are heavily right-skewed with a few mansions. The honest summary pair is…
steps.explanation: Skew + outliers → resistant pair: median and IQR. Mean ± SD would be dragged AND inflated by the mansions; the mixed pair (option 3) mismatches sensitivities and confuses everyone.

steps.title: Most data within 2s of the mean
steps.body: For reasonably bell-shaped data, roughly 95% of values sit within 2 SDs of the mean (Unit 10 makes this precise). Quick audit for any computed s: if barely anything or absolutely everything falls in x̄ ± 2s, recheck the arithmetic.

steps.title: Weather 'normals' are means with hidden SDs
steps.body: A city's 'normal high' for a date is a 30-year mean — but forecasters' famous caveat, 'near seasonal norms', hides an SD of several degrees. Two cities can share a normal of 20°C while one swings ±3° and the other ±12°. Travelers who pack by the mean alone discover standard deviation empirically.

steps.title: What you learned
points: s = √[Σ(x−x̄)²/(n−1)]: typical distance from the mean, in data units.
Shifts leave s alone; scaling by k scales s by |k|.
Matched pairs: mean↔SD, median↔IQR — never cross the streams.

lessons.title: Shape & Choosing Summaries
lessons.concreteComparison: Reaction times from a game: most players cluster around $0.3$ seconds, but a long tail of distracted players stretches past $2$ seconds. The mean says $0.51$; the median says $0.32$. Neither is wrong — but the SHAPE (that long right tail) decides which one belongs in the headline, and shape is the first thing a professional looks at, before any number gets computed.
lessons.objective: Classify distribution shapes (symmetric, skewed, uniform, bimodal), predict mean-median order from shape, and choose the right summary pair.
concept: **Shape vocabulary:** symmetric (mirror-image), right/positively skewed (long HIGH tail), left/negatively skewed (long LOW tail), uniform (flat), bimodal (two humps — often two mixed populations).

**Mean vs median order follows the tail:** the mean is dragged TOWARD the long tail — right skew pulls mean above median; left skew pulls it below; symmetry aligns them. Given two centers, you can infer the shape; given the shape, predict the order.

**Choice rules:** symmetric and outlier-free → mean ± SD (uses all information). Skewed or outlier-bearing → median + IQR (resistant). Bimodal → STOP: no single center describes two humps — split the populations and describe each.

The full description ritual (every dataset, every time): **S**hape, **C**enter, **S**pread, **O**utliers — in that order, in context, with units. Numbers without shape commentary are half a description.
lessons.keyIdea: The tail drags the mean: skew right → mean > median. Symmetric → mean±SD; skewed → median+IQR; bimodal → split before summarizing.

facts.title: Tail drags mean
facts.explanation: The mean chases the long tail; the median stays with the crowd.

facts.title: The description ritual
facts.explanation: Four items, in order, in context — a complete description.

workedExamples.statement: Reaction times: median $0.32$s, mean $0.51$s. Infer the shape and choose the summary pair.
workedExamples.solution: Mean well above median → a long HIGH tail: right-skewed (slow stragglers). Choice: median + IQR — "typical reaction $0.32$s" honestly describes the crowd; the mean's $0.51$ describes nobody in particular.

workedExamples.statement: Exam scores where most students did well but a few bombed: predict the shape and the mean-median order.
workedExamples.solution: Cluster high, tail stretching LOW: left-skewed. The mean chases the low tail: mean < median. (A class with median $85$ and mean $78$ is telling you exactly this story.)

workedExamples.statement: Heights of a mixed group of adults and their young children form two humps. What goes wrong if you report one mean, and what's the right move?
workedExamples.solution: The mean lands BETWEEN the humps — a height describing neither adults nor kids (nobody is $1.35$m in a room of $1.7$m adults and $1.0$m children). Bimodality means two populations: split by group, describe each with its own shape-center-spread.

commonMistakes.correction: Shape first — a dotplot or histogram sketch takes 30 seconds and decides which numbers are even meaningful. Bimodal data makes every single-center summary wrong.
Skew names the TAIL side, not the hump side: right-skewed data has its hump on the LEFT and tail stretching right. Say 'long tail to the ___' and the name follows.

tryIt.statement: A dataset has mean $42$ and median $42.1$. What shape do you infer, and which summary pair fits?
tryIt.solution: Centers essentially equal → roughly symmetric (and likely outlier-free): mean ± SD.

tryIt.statement: Household incomes: mean $4.8$M₮, median $3.1$M₮. Shape? Headline number?
tryIt.solution: Mean far above median: right skew (the usual income shape). Headline: the median — "typical household: $3.1$M₮".

steps.title: Shape before numbers
beats: Reaction times: crowd at $0.3$s, stragglers past $2$s.
Mean $0.51$, median $0.32$ — the SHAPE explains the gap.
Professionals sketch the distribution before computing anything.
Shape decides which numbers deserve the headline.

steps.title: Five shapes, one rule
beats: Symmetric · right-skewed (long HIGH tail) · left-skewed · uniform · bimodal.
Skew names the TAIL, not the hump.
The mean chases the tail; the median stays with the crowd.
So: right skew → mean > median. Left → mean < median. You can infer either way.

steps.title: Shape lives in the bins
steps.teach: Twenty values, three bin widths. At width 1 the picture is noise; at width 4 it's a blur; at width 2 the story appears — a pile on the left with a long right tail. Skewed-right data like this is exactly where you reach for the median and IQR, because the tail drags the mean.

steps.title: Read the gap
steps.prompt: City apartment rents: mean $2.4$M₮, median $1.8$M₮. The distribution is…
options: Right-skewed — luxury units stretch the high tail
Symmetric with outliers on both sides
steps.explanation: Mean $33\%$ above median: dragged by a long HIGH tail of luxury rents. (Symmetric two-sided outliers would cancel in the mean.) Rents, incomes, wait times — right skew is the default shape of bounded-below data.

steps.title: Pick the pair
steps.prompt: Dataset: 200 marathon finish times, right-skewed with a few walkers past 6 hours. Summarize with…
steps.explanation: Skew + high outliers → resistant pair. Mean ± SD would let a handful of walkers claim the headline; mode + range pairs the least informative center with the most fragile spread.

steps.title: SCSO: Shape, Center, Spread, Outliers
steps.body: Every data description, every time, in order: SHAPE (in words), CENTER (the right one, with units), SPREAD (its partner), OUTLIERS (flagged and investigated). Four sentences make a complete, professional description — and Unit 12's comparisons will run the same ritual twice.

steps.title: The bimodal bus stop
steps.body: Traffic engineers discovered bus-stop waiting times are often bimodal: one hump of people who check the schedule (short waits) and one of people who don't (uniform waits). Averaging them produces a 'typical wait' that describes neither strategy — a textbook case of split-before-summarizing, live at your nearest stop.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Shape first: symmetric, skewed (named by the TAIL), uniform, bimodal.
The mean chases the tail — infer shape from center order and back.
Symmetric → mean±SD; skewed → median+IQR; bimodal → split. SCSO always.

lessons.title: Capstone: Complete Descriptions
lessons.concreteComparison: Your school's two math sections took the same exam. Section 1: median $74$, IQR $12$, symmetric, no outliers. Section 2: median $74$, IQR $13$ — but left-skewed with three flagged scores below $30$. "Both sections performed identically" says the lazy summary. The complete description says: same typical performance, but Section 2 has three students in trouble RIGHT NOW. Full descriptions aren't pedantry; they're where the decisions live.
lessons.objective: Produce complete SCSO descriptions and side-by-side comparisons, choosing summaries deliberately and connecting them to decisions.
concept: A complete single-group description runs SCSO in context: shape (words), center (the matched one, with units), spread (its partner), outliers (flagged by the fence, investigated, dispositioned). Every sentence names the variable and units — "scores", not "the data".

A complete COMPARISON runs the ritual in parallel: compare centers (who's typically higher), spreads (who's more consistent), shapes (who has which tail), outliers (who has stragglers) — four contrasts, each earning one sentence.

Summaries serve DECISIONS: the outlier list is a to-do list (three students below $30$ need help today); the IQR picks the more consistent supplier; the skew warns which tail your plan must survive. End every description with the "so what" sentence.

The unit's toolbox, assembled: two centers, two spreads, quartiles, fences, boxplots, shape vocabulary, and matched-pair discipline. Unit 10 adds position (percentiles, z-scores) and the bell curve; Units 11–12 put data pairs and honest conclusions on top.
lessons.keyIdea: SCSO in context, matched pairs, fence-flagged outliers with dispositions, and a closing 'so what' — that's a complete description; comparisons run it in parallel.

facts.title: Single group
facts.explanation: Shape, center, spread, outliers — in context, ending in a decision.

facts.title: Two groups
facts.explanation: The same ritual, run in parallel, one sentence each.

workedExamples.statement: Commute times (min): $18, 22, 24, 25, 26, 28, 30, 31, 34, 62$. Produce a complete description.
workedExamples.solution: $n = 10$, sorted. Median $= \frac{26+28}{2} = 27$; $Q_1 = 24, Q_3 = 31$, IQR $= 7$; fences $13.5$ and $41.5$ → the $62$ is flagged. Shape: apart from the flag, roughly symmetric. Description: "Commutes center near $27$ min (median), middle half spanning $24$–$31$ ($\text{IQR } 7$); one flagged outlier at $62$ min — investigated: a snowstorm day. Typical planning number: $27 \pm$ a few minutes; keep the storm day in the record, noted."

workedExamples.statement: Two suppliers' delivery delays (days): S1 mean $3.1$, SD $0.4$, symmetric. S2 mean $2.8$, SD $1.9$, right-skewed with flagged $9$- and $11$-day delays. Which supplier, and why — in full-comparison form?
workedExamples.solution: Centers: S2 typically faster ($2.8 < 3.1$). Spreads: S1 five times more consistent ($0.4$ vs $1.9$). Shapes: S2's right skew means its bad days are VERY bad; S1 has no tail. Outliers: S2 owns the $9$ and $11$-day disasters. Decision: production lines that stop when parts are late should pay S1's extra $0.3$ days for the consistency; a warehouse with buffer stock can bank S2's speed.

workedExamples.statement: The two exam sections (both median $74$; S2 left-skewed with three scores below $30$). Write the two sentences the principal actually needs.
workedExamples.solution: "Both sections perform identically at the typical-student level (median $74$, similar IQRs). Section 2 additionally has three students scoring below $30$ — flagged outliers needing intervention now, whom the identical medians completely hide." The summary statistic answered 'how are classes doing'; the outlier list answered 'who needs help' — different questions, both in the data.

commonMistakes.text: Describing numbers instead of the situation.
commonMistakes.correction: "Median 27, IQR 7" is a fragment. "Commutes typically run 27 minutes, with the middle half between 24 and 31" is a description. Context and units transform statistics into information.
Equal medians hid three failing students. Comparisons run all four contrasts — centers, spreads, shapes, outliers — because decisions hide in every one.

tryIt.statement: Data: $5, 6, 6, 7, 8, 9, 30$. Run the mechanics: median, quartiles, IQR, fences, flags.
tryIt.solution: Median $= 7$; $Q_1 = 6$, $Q_3 = 9$ (halves exclude the median), IQR $= 3$; fences $1.5$ and $13.5$; the $30$ is flagged. Shape apart from the flag: mildly right-leaning, roughly symmetric core.

tryIt.statement: Team A quiz scores: mean $7.5$, SD $0.5$. Team B: mean $7.5$, SD $2.5$. Write the one-sentence comparison a coach needs.
tryIt.solution: "Both teams average the same, but B swings five times more — A is the reliable performer; B owns both the stars and the liabilities."

steps.title: Identical medians, hidden crisis
beats: Two sections, both median $74$. "Identical performance."
Except Section 2 hides three students below $30$.
The lazy summary was TRUE and useless.
Complete descriptions are where decisions live.

steps.title: SCSO, in context, with a 'so what'
beats: Shape in words. Center — the matched one, with units.
Spread — its partner. Outliers — flagged, investigated, dispositioned.
Every sentence names the variable: "commutes", not "the data".
Close with the decision the description feeds.

steps.title: Whose spread matters?
steps.prompt: An ambulance service picks between two routes with equal MEAN times. Route X: SD $1$ min. Route Y: SD $6$ min. The service should…
options: Take X — for emergencies, the worst case rules, and Y's tail is deadly
Take Y — same mean, so flip a coin
Take Y — bigger spread means more fast days too
steps.explanation: Y's spread does include faster days — and 12-minutes-late days, which in this context cost lives. When downside tails carry the cost, consistency IS the metric. Spread comparisons are decisions wearing arithmetic.

steps.title: What's missing?
steps.prompt: A report reads: "Group 1 median 50, IQR 10; Group 2 median 58, IQR 11." Which contrast is MISSING that could reverse the conclusion?
options: Shapes and outliers — a flagged cluster or heavy skew could change everything
steps.explanation: Two of four contrasts reported. If Group 2's extra 8 points come with a left tail of failing members (like Section 2), 'Group 2 is better' collapses for exactly the people who need attention. Four contrasts, always.

steps.title: You now describe; next you locate
steps.body: Unit 9's kit: centers, spreads, quartiles, fences, boxplots, shapes, matched pairs, SCSO. Unit 10 adds the question this unit can't answer: how good is ONE value inside its distribution — percentiles, z-scores, and the bell curve that makes 'usual' precise.

steps.title: Anscombe's quartet
steps.body: In 1973 Francis Anscombe built four datasets with IDENTICAL means, variances, and correlations — whose graphs look wildly different: a line, a curve, an outlier-pivot, and a vertical stack. His point is this unit's point: summaries without shape are half-truths. Plot first; the numbers follow.

steps.title: What you learned — Unit 9 complete
points: Complete description: SCSO + context + units + 'so what'.
Comparisons: four contrasts in parallel — centers, spreads, shapes, outliers.
Summaries feed decisions; outlier lists are to-do lists. Next: position and the bell.

practice.statement: Find the mean and median of $12, 15, 11, 18, 14$.
Data: $8, 9, 10, 11, 62$. Compute both centers and say which describes a typical value.
practice.solution: Mean $= \frac{100}{5} = 20$ (dragged); median $= 10$. The median — four of five values sit within $2$ of it.

practice.statement: Sorted data: $10, 14, 15, 17, 20, 22, 28$. Find the five-number summary and IQR.
With $Q_1 = 40$ and $Q_3 = 60$, which of these values are outliers by the fence rule: $5, 12, 88, 95$?
practice.solution: IQR $= 20$; fences at $10$ and $90$. Flagged: $5$ (below $10$) and $95$ (above $90$). The $12$ and $88$ are inside.

practice.statement: Every value in a dataset with mean $30$ and SD $5$ is multiplied by $3$. New mean and SD?
A dataset has mean $58$ and median $45$. Infer the shape and pick the summary pair.
practice.solution: Mean well above median: right-skewed. Report median + IQR.

practice.statement: Two cashiers' service times: X mean $2.0$ min SD $0.2$; Y mean $1.8$ min SD $1.5$. Who serves a line of $30$ customers more predictably, and who is faster on average?
practice.solution: Y is faster on average ($1.8 < 2.0$); X is vastly more predictable (SD $0.2$ vs $1.5$). Queue management usually pays the $0.2$ min for X's consistency.

testYourself.statement: Data: $6, 8, 5, 9, 6, 40$. Compute mean and median, name the better center, and give the shape implied.
testYourself.solution: Mean $= \frac{74}{6} = 12.33$; sorted $5,6,6,8,9,40$: median $= 7$. Median wins (the $40$ drags the mean above every other value); mean ≫ median implies right skew — here driven by a single outlier.

testYourself.statement: Sorted commute data ($n = 9$): $15, 18, 20, 22, 24, 27, 30, 33, 51$. Five-number summary, IQR, fences, flags.
Compute the sample SD of $10, 12, 14, 16, 18$, and interpret it in a sentence.
testYourself.solution: $\bar{x} = 14$; squared deviations $16, 4, 0, 4, 16$ sum to $40$; $s = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$. "Values typically sit about $3$ units from the mean of $14$."

testYourself.statement: A teacher adds $5$ bonus points to every student's score (mean was $71$, SD $8$, median $73$, IQR $10$). Report all four updated statistics.
testYourself.solution: Everything shifts, nothing spreads: mean $76$, median $78$, SD still $8$, IQR still $10$.

testYourself.statement: Which is larger for hospital emergency-room waiting times (a right-skewed distribution): the mean or the median? Whose planning question does each answer?
testYourself.solution: The mean exceeds the median (dragged by long-wait tail). The median answers the PATIENT's question ("how long will I probably wait?"); the mean answers the ADMINISTRATOR's ("total patient-hours per shift = mean × count" — staffing and cost live on the mean).

testYourself.statement: Two archery teams at trials — P: mean $8.2$, SD $0.3$, symmetric, no flags. Q: mean $8.4$, SD $1.8$, left-skewed, two flagged scores of $3$. One team advances to a knockout final where every arrow must score $7+$. Write the complete comparison and the pick.
testYourself.solution: Centers: Q higher by $0.2$. Spreads: P six times tighter. Shapes: Q's left skew means its failures are severe; P has none. Outliers: Q's two $3$s are exactly the knockout-losing arrows. Pick P: in an every-arrow-counts format, Q's tail is fatal and P's floor (~$7.6$ at $2\sigma$) clears the bar. The 'better average' team loses this format.
